discovery.text: From Allan B. Jacobs' Great Streets (1993). One of the finest examples of a North American boulevard. The long tree-lined mall from the Public Garden through Back Bay is Jacobs' proof that American cities can do it right.

narrative.summary: One of the finest examples of a North American boulevard. The long tree-lined mall from the Public Garden through Back Bay is Jacobs' proof that American cities can do it right.

blocks.title: Why Jacobs Chose It
blocks.body: 240-foot wide right-of-way with a central landscaped mall, Victorian brownstones on both sides maintaining consistent scale, mature American elms (replanted after Dutch elm disease), active ground floors. Walking the full length from Arlington St to Kenmore Sq is a full urban experience.

blocks.title: How to Walk It
blocks.body: Walk the full length. Jacobs is explicit: you have to walk a great street to understand it — the sequence, the proportions, how the edges change. Give it an hour minimum. Sit at a café or on a bench midway. Notice what makes it feel alive.

blocks.name: Buttermilk & Bourbon
blocks.why: Right on Commonwealth Ave in the heart of Back Bay. Southern food and cocktails in a brownstone storefront — the active ground floor that keeps Jacobs' great street fed and inhabited.

blocks.name: Deuxave
blocks.why: Named for 2 Av — the address says it all. Fine dining in a brownstone on Commonwealth's mall section. Where the street dresses up; the kind of institution Jacobs sees anchoring a great boulevard's residential blocks.

blocks.title: The Mall Statues
blocks.body: Walk the central mall and look for the Leif Eriksson statue, the Patrick Collins memorial, and Samuel Eliot Morison seated on a boulder. These monuments break up the long axis and give the walker reasons to stop — exactly the punctuation Jacobs praises in great pedestrian corridors.
